author: @jTheN77
text:
A big part of the movie industry "problem" is that the cost of any film relies greatly on big pay checks paid to the upper crust of the cast and crew (the managerial crew that is, not the people doing most of the real work). They get paid no matter what. Their paycheck remains in their bank no matter how badly the movie performs - if no profit is made, investors may suffer and anyone putting money into the movie in hopes of a payout may suffer and people at the lower rungs who work "9 to 5" may not have a job for a while. But, the con artists up top keep getting paid money that any ordinary person would faint over. They keep pushing large wads of dough into their accounts. They have plenty of money for retirement; anything extra is just another vacation house or two or cruise around the world, some other frivolity ordinary people cannot even dream of. So, yeah, it is a shame that people do not appreciate their box office cow flops more. But, still, ask them how much money they have in the bank? And if they aren't griping with sour grapes over not having ten or twenty million more in the banks, darn it.

In other words: "We got the money, sucker. If you want a decent movie to watch, make your own!"
